m HE oldest photograph of Spo-


kane extant, antedating any


- picture of the city hitherto
brought to light, was identified re-
Gently by oldtimers following its
being brought to one of the public
schools of the city by pupil in
an "early history of Spokane" class.
The photograph was believed to
have been a view of Riverside ave-
nue., but turned out to be one Of
Howard street, looking south from
the corner of Front avenue and
Howard street. 1t is probably one
of the most valuable relics of the
pioneer days of Spokane Falls in
existence.


The photograph, which is repro
duced herewith, was brought to
school by a pupil, Ellen Mason, in
the class of Miss Galley at the
Hamilton school. She said the
had been given to her
brother and it was not then be-
lieved to have any great value.


Taken in the winter or spring
sometime between 1881 and 1888, it
shows Howard street covered with
